title: Design a URL Shortener
interviewPrompt: For today's Software Engineer interview, design a URL shortener.

beats.interviewer: For today's Software Engineer interview, design a URL shortener.
beats.interviewee: High level, three moves: take a long URL, hand back a short code, and redirect on lookup. Simplest version, hash the URL, store the code-to-URL mapping in a key-value store, and redirect on the way back.
beats.show: A short link is just a lookup: code in, long URL out.

beats.interviewer: Say it gets popular. What breaks first?
beats.interviewee: Two things. A hash like MD5 is too long, and if I truncate it, it collides. And at a few thousand writes a second, that single row insert on the hot path becomes the ceiling.
beats.show: Hash is too long, truncating collides, and the write is the bottleneck.

beats.interviewer: Let's go deeper on the code generation. Fix it.
beats.interviewee: Don't hash at all. Hand out a unique 64-bit ID from a counter, then base62-encode it. Seven characters gives 3.5 trillion links, and now the redirect is a single key lookup.
beats.show: Counter → base62. Seven chars = 3.5 trillion links.

beats.interviewer: Is a central counter the only option?
beats.interviewee: No, and that's the real tradeoff. Want codes unguessable? Add a salt or a Hashids permutation. Can't tolerate a central counter? Pre-allocate ID ranges per server.
beats.show: Not the only way: salt for privacy, or pre-allocate ID ranges per server.

beats.interviewee: Reads beat writes about 100 to 1, so the whole job is making that redirect a cache hit. Keep the mapping in a key-value store like DynamoDB, and cache the hot links in Redis so the hot path never touches it.
beats.show: Reads ≫ writes. Mapping in DynamoDB, hot links cached in Redis.
